questions.question: Define electric current and potential difference. State the relationship between them.
questions.answer: Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor (I = Q/t). Potential difference is the work done in moving a unit positive charge from one point to another in an electric field (V = W/Q). According to Ohm's law, current is directly proportional to potential difference at constant temperature: V = IR.

questions.question: State Ohm's law. Draw a V-I graph for an ohmic conductor and explain its significance.
questions.answer: Ohm's law: The current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across its ends at constant temperature (V = IR). The V-I graph is a straight line passing through the origin. The slope of the graph gives the resistance R of the conductor.

questions.question: Describe an experiment to verify Ohm's law in the laboratory.
questions.answer: Apparatus: battery, rheostat, ammeter, voltmeter, key, resistor. Connect in series (ammeter) and parallel (voltmeter across resistor). Vary current using rheostat, note V and I readings. Plot V vs I graph. Straight line through origin confirms Ohm's law; slope = R.

questions.question: Explain the factors affecting the resistance of a conductor.
questions.answer: Resistance depends on: (i) length (R ∝ l), (ii) cross-sectional area (R ∝ 1/A), (iii) nature of material (resistivity ρ), (iv) temperature (increases in metals). The formula is R = ρl/A.

questions.question: Derive the expression for equivalent resistance of resistors connected in series.
questions.answer: In series, same current I passes through each resistor. Total potential difference V = V1 + V2 + V3 = IR1 + IR2 + IR3 = I(R1 + R2 + R3). Hence, equivalent resistance Req = R1 + R2 + R3.

questions.question: Derive the expression for equivalent resistance of resistors connected in parallel.
questions.answer: In parallel, same potential difference V across each. Total current I = I1 + I2 + I3 = V/R1 + V/R2 + V/R3. Hence, 1/Req = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3.

questions.question: State two advantages of connecting electrical appliances in parallel in domestic circuits.
questions.answer: (i) Each appliance gets the same voltage (rated value) and operates independently. (ii) If one appliance fails or is switched off, others continue to work normally (circuit not broken). This is why household wiring uses parallel combination.

questions.question: Explain Joule's law of heating. Mention two practical applications.
questions.answer: Heat produced in a conductor is directly proportional to square of current, resistance, and time: H = I²Rt. Applications: (i) Electric iron, heater, toaster (high-resistance coil). (ii) Electric fuse (low melting wire melts on high current).

questions.question: Define electric power. Derive the expressions P = VI, P = I²R and P = V²/R.
questions.answer: Electric power is the rate at which electrical energy is consumed or dissipated. P = Work/time = VQ/t = VI. Using V = IR: P = I × IR = I²R; and P = V × (V/R) = V²/R.

questions.question: What is the commercial unit of electrical energy? How is an electricity bill calculated?
questions.answer: The commercial unit is kilowatt-hour (kWh), also called 1 unit. 1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6 × 10^6 J. Electricity consumed (units) = Power (kW) × time (h). Bill = units × cost per unit.

questions.question: Why is nichrome used as a heating element in electric appliances?
questions.answer: Nichrome has high resistivity (produces more heat for given current), high melting point (does not melt easily), and does not oxidise readily at high temperatures. It is therefore suitable for heaters, toasters, and irons.

questions.question: Explain the working of an electric fuse in a household circuit.
questions.answer: Fuse contains a thin wire of low melting point. During overload or short circuit, current increases, wire heats up (H = I²Rt) and melts, breaking the circuit and preventing fire or damage. Fuse is connected in live wire.

questions.question: Explain the concept of resistivity and its unit.
questions.answer: Resistivity (ρ) is the resistance offered by a unit length and unit cross-sectional area of a material (ρ = R × A / l). It is a material property, independent of dimensions. Unit: ohm-metre (Ω m).

questions.question: Why is electrical power transmitted at high voltage over long distances?
questions.answer: Power loss in transmission lines = I²R. For same power P = VI, high V means low I, reducing I²R loss. Step-up transformers increase voltage for transmission; step-down for distribution.

questions.question: Differentiate between ohmic and non-ohmic conductors with examples and V-I graphs.
questions.answer: Ohmic conductors obey Ohm's law (V ∝ I), straight V-I line (e.g., copper wire). Non-ohmic do not obey (V not proportional to I), curved V-I graph (e.g., filament lamp — resistance increases with temperature).

questions.question: Explain how to find equivalent resistance in a mixed series-parallel circuit with an example.
questions.answer: Identify parallel groups first, find their equivalent, then add series parts. Example: 3Ω and 6Ω in parallel (Rp = 2Ω), then in series with 4Ω → Req = 2 + 4 = 6Ω.

questions.question: What is earthing? Why is it essential in domestic wiring?
questions.answer: Earthing connects the metal body of appliances to the ground via a low-resistance wire. It provides a safe path for leakage current to flow to earth, preventing electric shock to the user.

questions.question: Explain the heating effect of electric current and give its formula.
questions.answer: When current flows through a conductor, it collides with atoms, producing heat. Heat produced H = I²Rt (Joule's law of heating). Heat is proportional to square of current, resistance, and time.

questions.question: Why is copper preferred over aluminium for household electrical wiring?
questions.answer: Copper has lower resistivity than aluminium, so less power loss (I²R). It is more ductile, corrosion-resistant, and better conductor. Though aluminium is cheaper and lighter, copper is safer and more efficient for internal wiring.

questions.question: Describe the main components of a domestic electric circuit with a labelled diagram.
questions.answer: Components: live wire (red), neutral wire (black), earth wire (green), main switch, fuse/MCB, distribution board, parallel connections for appliances. Earth wire connected to ground for safety.

questions.question: Compare the working of a fuse and an MCB in household circuits.
questions.answer: Fuse: thin low-melting wire melts on overload, needs replacement. MCB: electromagnet or bimetallic strip trips switch on overload, can be reset. MCB is more convenient, faster, and reusable.

questions.question: A 100 W bulb is operated at 220 V. Calculate the current drawn and resistance of the filament.
questions.answer: Power P = VI ⇒ I = P/V = 100/220 ≈ 0.455 A. Resistance R = V²/P = 220²/100 = 484 Ω. The filament has high resistance to produce heat and light.

questions.question: Explain why resistance of a conductor increases with temperature.
questions.answer: In metals, as temperature rises, atoms vibrate more, increasing collisions with free electrons. This reduces drift velocity, increasing resistance. Resistance R = R0(1 + αΔT), where α is temperature coefficient.

questions.question: Explain the importance of using a three-pin plug for electrical appliances.
questions.answer: Three-pin plug has live, neutral, and earth pins. Earth pin connects appliance body to ground, providing path for leakage current, preventing shock. Longer earth pin ensures earthing before live/neutral contact.

questions.question: Explain why electric bulbs are filled with inert gas or vacuum.
questions.answer: Tungsten filament operates at very high temperature (~2500°C). In air, it would oxidise and burn out quickly. Vacuum or inert gas (argon/nitrogen) prevents oxidation, allowing longer life and brighter light.
